question: Based on Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.2-2, is the operating pressure adequately protected by the reactor safety valves?

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section explains that the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and states that the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC is the code of record for the design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0111.title: Figure 1.7-2: Instrumentatio n and Controls Symbol Legend
nuscale_ch01_0111.summary: This section provides a legend explaining the symbols used for instrumentation and controls in NuScale’s safety analysis diagrams. It describes the logic gate functions, control room indicators, switch types, and other symbols relevant to system status and operations, ensuring clear interpretation of the diagrams. Additionally, it includes notes on piping and instrumentation diagram conventions, such as vent and drain representations.

nuscale_ch01_0112.title: Figure 1.7-3a: Piping and Instrumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0112.summary: This section provides the legend for symbols used in NuScale’s Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), detailing the graphical representations for various valves, fire and safety equipment, and specialty items. It specifies standard conventions, such as all vents and drains being represented by a 0.75" normally closed valve unless otherwise noted, and includes notes on how to interpret flow paths and system abbreviations.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life, in accordance with regulatory definitions. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, emphasizing their roles in maintaining pressure containment and system safety.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including its main systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific information, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI). Security-related details are specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, and features a small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design. It relies on passive safety systems—including dedicated emergency core cooling and decay heat removal systems—that do not require electrical power, and its components are partially immersed in a reactor pool for enhanced safety and heat removal. The NPM allows for flexible power maneuvering using control rods and soluble boron, with all major components integrated within the reactor pressure vessel to minimize external piping and penetrations.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations. Instrumentation provides continuous monitoring of pressure and water level to support safe operation.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards related to the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases relevant to reactor component materials, inspection, and quality assurance.

nuscale_ch01_0769.title: SRP 15.6.1, Rev 2: Inadvertent Opening of a PWR Pressurizer Relief Valve or a BWR Pressure Relief ValveAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0769.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 15.6.1, which addresses the inadvertent opening of pressurizer relief valves, because it does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs) that are susceptible to such failures. Instead, NuScale uses spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, and any mechanical failure of these valves is addressed by analyses of inadvertent ECCS valve actuation in another section. Thus, the specific guidance for PORVs is not fully applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0113.title: Figure 1.7-3b: Piping and In strumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0113.summary: This section provides the legend for Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, detailing standard symbols and notations for valve positions, actuator types, and operational statuses. It explains abbreviations such as NO (normally open), NC (normally closed), FO (fail open), and describes various actuator mechanisms and their functions for clarity in interpreting P&IDs.

nuscale_ch01_0767.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.4.B Input Parameters and Initial Conditions - Scram Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0767.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for chemical and volume control system malfunctions that increase reactor coolant inventory, specifically addressing input parameters and initial conditions such as scram characteristics and core burnup. It notes full conformance with DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2 requirements, and clarifies that certain SRP guidance related to pressurizer relief valves is only partially applicable due to differences in NuScale’s design, which uses spring-loaded safety valves instead of power-operated relief valves.

nuscale_ch01_0039.title: Technical Support Center
nuscale_ch01_0039.summary: The section describes the implementation of digital control systems in the NuScale plant, ensuring independence between safety and nonsafety systems, and providing operators with comprehensive monitoring and control capabilities through human-factored displays. It also notes the presence of a Technical Support Center (TSC) compliant with regulatory requirements, and briefly mentions the Radioactive Waste Building (RWB) for processing and preparing radioactive waste for shipment.

nuscale_ch01_0160.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.25 Design and Procurement Specifications
nuscale_ch01_0160.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment, as well as design and procurement specifications. It notes that certain requirements, such as DSRS 3.11 II.25, are not applicable to NuScale and are instead items for the COL applicant, while other standards (e.g., SRP 3.12 and 3.13) are confirmed as met. Additionally, guidance specific to BWR plants is marked as not applicable to NuScale’s design.

edges.description: Section A describes compliance with regulatory codes for reactor pressure boundary integrity, while Section B provides definitions for instrumentation and control logic symbols, which are used to implement and verify the safety functions required by those codes.

Section A establishes compliance with regulatory codes for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B provides the legend for interpreting piping and instrumentation diagrams, which is necessary to understand the specific safety valve configurations referenced in the analysis question.

Section A states that the reactor pressure vessel and pressure-retaining components are designed and constructed in compliance with ASME Code and regulatory requirements, while Section B describes the design features that maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, thus Section A provides the regulatory compliance details that support the general integrity claims made in Section B.

Section A provides the logic symbols and control logic definitions used in instrumentation diagrams, which are necessary to interpret the safety system logic that protects the reactor coolant boundary described in Section B.

Section A references the design and procurement specifications for mechanical and electrical equipment, while Section B details compliance with ASME Code and regulatory requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, connecting them through the shared requirement that equipment and components (including safety valves) must meet specified codes and standards to ensure adequate protection of operating pressure.

Section A outlines the regulatory and design specification requirements for mechanical and electrical equipment, while Section B provides the legend necessary to interpret the piping and instrumentation diagrams that would show the placement and type of reactor safety valves, thus Section B provides the detailed diagrammatic context needed to verify if the design in Section A is implemented correctly.
